candidates.name: Multi-provider fallback generator (iterProviders)
candidates.notes: Generator yields provider clients in preference order; for...of catches per-provider errors and advances. Small port (~40 lines). Reuse targets: deep-research cross-model verification, future multi-SERP surface, media-analysis transcript providers, embedding providers.
candidates.rank_rationale: High relevance + low effort + concrete reuse across existing SoNash skills. Top T1 item.

candidates.name: x-* request header protocol
candidates.notes: Runtime config surface via headers (11+ headers). Cleaner than query-string pollution. Applicable when CAS exposes /website-analysis over MCP or as agent-facing tool surface.
candidates.rank_rationale: Design reference — not a port — but likely useful when MCP surface emerges for CAS skills.

candidates.name: r.jina.ai hosted service as fetch backend
candidates.notes: DECISION candidate: adopt as /website-analysis fallback fetch backend? Pro: free tier, TLS fingerprint evasion, SPA handling. Con: vendor lock-in on core CAS primitive, 11-month staleness, Privacy-First tension. Recommended next step: instrument superpowers-chrome gaps first.
candidates.rank_rationale: Highest-stakes decision surfaced by this analysis. Effort E0 because it's a call-or-don't-call decision.

candidates.name: Tiered fetch fallback chain with sideLoad body hint
candidates.notes: Firestore cache -> curl -> proxy retry -> Puppeteer with sideLoad hint -> Readability -> Turndown -> stale cache fallback. Reference for /website-analysis pipeline evolution beyond superpowers-chrome.
candidates.rank_rationale: Medium relevance, moderate effort — file for later if /website-analysis needs production-grade extraction.

candidates.name: Readability + Turndown + Puppeteer extraction pipeline
candidates.notes: Industry-standard HTML->markdown stack. All stages swappable via DI. If /website-analysis needs non-Chrome fallback (static sites only), HTML + @mozilla/readability covers happy path with small dep footprint.
candidates.rank_rationale: Low novelty (standard pattern) but genuinely reusable. Park for when superpowers-chrome alternative is needed.

candidates.name: curl-impersonate + TLS fingerprint concept
candidates.notes: Understanding that anti-bot defenses fingerprint the full TLS/TCP stack explains why firecrawl/crawl4ai leak through some sites. Not directly usable in SoNash (no self-hosted fetcher), but conceptually T2.
candidates.rank_rationale: Conceptual knowledge with indirect applicability. Worth understanding for any future content-fetch primitive in JASON-OS.

candidates.name: curl-impersonate as Docker LD_PRELOAD base layer
candidates.notes: Two-stage Docker build: FROM lwthiker/curl-impersonate ... -> FROM node:22. Copy .so + LD_PRELOAD. Template for any future self-hosted fetcher that needs to defeat anti-bot TLS fingerprinting.
candidates.rank_rationale: High effort (Docker + C lib + system-level config). Only relevant if SoNash ever containerizes a fetcher. T3 reference.

candidates.name: Multi-target deployment via --args override
candidates.notes: One image deployed 6 times to Cloud Run by varying --args. DI resolves correct class at container boot. Not directly applicable (SoNash is Firebase Functions), but reference pattern if JASON-OS needs a polyglot service shell.
candidates.rank_rationale: Low immediate relevance — SoNash doesn't use Cloud Run. File as architecture pattern reference.

candidates.name: Commercial OSS without tests as an architectural stance
candidates.notes: Reader runs at scale with zero tests. Quality floor = TypeScript + lint + observability + traffic volume. A defensible stance for the originator but does not transfer to adopters. Cautionary knowledge.
candidates.rank_rationale: Evergreen reference — don't emulate, but understand the trade-off when evaluating similar codebases.

candidates.name: Blog: Reader for search-grounding to improve factuality of LLMs
candidates.notes: Explains s.jina.ai's thesis — search-then-read for LLM grounding. Aligned with SoNash deep-research philosophy. Validation rather than novelty.
candidates.rank_rationale: Low-effort content read. Validates existing CAS approach.

candidates.name: Colab notebook: full-site crawling methodology
candidates.notes: The only practical multi-URL example using r.jina.ai. Useful IF /website-analysis adds a bulk mode later. Not urgent.
candidates.rank_rationale: Filed for future multi-URL use case. Evergreen reference.

candidates.name: DeepWiki-as-architecture-docs
candidates.notes: README 'How it works' is a DeepWiki image badge. No first-party architecture exposition. Directly anti-example for SoNash docs philosophy. Check docs/agent_docs/* — none of these should ever outsource architecture narrative.
candidates.rank_rationale: Active anti-pattern — immediately applicable as a 'do not do this' reference for SoNash documentation standards.

candidates.name: Public repo + private submodule requirement
candidates.notes: Public repo doesn't compile without SSH-only thinapps-shared. README calls it 'not integral' — misleading. Lesson: if shipping OSS, make it actually runnable from source. If closed, ship closed. Middle ground erodes trust.
candidates.rank_rationale: Active anti-pattern for any future SoNash OSS release or JASON-OS distribution.

candidates.name: Zero tests in a 10k-star production service
candidates.notes: Reader has no test suite. Quality = TypeScript strict + lint + traffic. Works for originator (observability), fails for adopters. Cautionary anti-pattern: 'no tests' requires compensating monitoring, not the other way around.
candidates.rank_rationale: Active cautionary reference — file as 'no tests' quality floor boundary marker for adoption decisions.

cross_repo_connections.detail: Closest analyzed analogue. Firecrawl is self-hostable with tests; Reader is not. Reader has TLS fingerprint evasion (curl-impersonate); Firecrawl does not. If adopting one as infrastructure, firecrawl wins on adopter-safety; Reader wins on hardened-fetcher output.

crawl4ai is Python, research-oriented, LLM-extraction focused. Reader is TypeScript, production-oriented, commercial. Different product slopes — Reader is what crawl4ai might look like if it commercialized.
Docling covers documents (PDF/DOCX). Reader's PDF handling via pdfjs-dist is a sidebar to web extraction. Do not overlap — CAS likely wants docling for documents and Reader/firecrawl for web.
Marker is PDF-to-markdown. Reader's PDF path is minimal. Different primary domains — marker is for document corpus conversion, Reader is for web content.
Reader depends on curl-impersonate as Docker base. Worth analyzing as a standalone repo if SoNash ever considers self-hosted fetching. Not yet analyzed.
Reader uses @mozilla/readability as its extraction core. Library-level dependency. Industry standard — Firefox Reader Mode is built on this. Not a priority to analyze as own repo.

synthesis_notes: Fourth web-content-extraction repo analyzed. Cross-repo synthesis should now have enough convergence evidence to produce a 'state of web content extraction' reading. Recommend running /synthesize scoped to these 4 repos to surface shared patterns (Readability + Turndown is universal) and divergences (TLS fingerprinting only in Reader; testing only in firecrawl).
